claim_reviews.claim: For A={1,2,3}, all integers d ≤ 10^{10} except the 27 listed (all ≤ 6234) are Zaremba-covered; the GPU computation took 12 hours on a DGX-B200 cluster.
claim_reviews.evidence: Enumerating every continued-fraction expansion with partial quotients in {1,2,3} and denominator ≤10^{10} is expected to require ≈N^{2δ}=10^{14.1} expansions (Hensley 1992). Processing ≈10^{14} items in 4.3×10^{4}s implies ~3×10^{9} expansions/s cluster-wide, far above the throughput of an 8-GPU DGX box. No description is given of pruning, hashing, or sieving sufficient to reduce work by 4–5 orders of magnitude, so the runtime claim and hence completeness beyond d=6 234 remain unsubstantiated.
claim_reviews.concerns: Clarify exact node count, GPU count, and algorithmic shortcuts; provide raw logs or checksums so others can replicate.

claim_reviews.evidence: The reported counts imply ≈7.2×10^{8} covered numbers, matching earlier independent computations by Bourgain & Kontorovich (2014, Fig. 3). Enumerating to 10^{9} with δ≈0.531 requires ~10^{9.06}≈1.2×10^{9} expansions, feasible in seconds on a modern GPU (≈10^{8}-10^{9} kernel calls/s).

claim_reviews.claim: Digit sets with Hausdorff dimension δ>1/2 but lacking digit 1 can still fail to reach full density; transitivity of the semigroup action is an additional necessary ingredient.
claim_reviews.evidence: Bourgain–Kontorovich 2014, §6–7, shows that spectral gap + strong primitivity (which implies transitivity mod p) is required; counter-examples with δ>1/2 but congruence obstructions are known. The experimental data for A={2,3,4,5} corroborates the theoretical expectation.

claim_reviews.claim: A={2,3,4,5} has Hausdorff dimension δ≈0.837 yet only 97.3 % density at d≤10^{9}.
claim_reviews.evidence: The manuscript elsewhere lists δ≈0.605 for the same set and gives contradictory density trajectories (97.3→97.1→98.8). Published computations (Jenkinson-Pollicott 2001) give δ≈0.605 for {2,3,4,5}. The figure 0.837 appears to be that of {1,2,3,4,5}. Hence the dimension quoted for {2,3,4,5} is inconsistent.
claim_reviews.concerns: Correct the Hausdorff dimension value and resolve the conflicting density numbers.

claim_reviews.claim: Of the 1 023 non-empty subsets of {1,…,10}, 366 achieve ≥99.99 % density at N=10^{6}, and 361 of those contain digit 1.
claim_reviews.evidence: A complete brute-force sweep to N=10^{6} requires ≤10^{7} expansions per subset for the worst cases, well within CPU bounds. Spot checks on the shared CSV reproduce the tallies. The combinatorial statement is internally consistent.

claim_reviews.claim: Minimum cardinality giving full density (0 uncovered in tested range) is 5 (e.g. A={1,2,3,4,5}).
claim_reviews.evidence: The dense sweep shows no 4-element subset reached 100 % to 10^{6}; this matches earlier computations by Hensley (1992) and BK (2014) which found sporadic misses for 4-element sets. Claim is consistent with dataset provided.
Values agree to 6-7 digits with high-precision transfer-operator calculations in Jenkinson–Pollicott 2001 and Hensley 1992.

claim_reviews.claim: Bourgain–Kontorovich (2014) proved density 1 for max digit 50 (non-effective).
Hensley's conjecture that δ>1/2 alone implies full density is false.
claim_reviews.evidence: Counter-examples with congruence obstructions (e.g. A={2,3,4}) are discussed in BK 2014; recent work of Magee–Oh (2023) formalises such failures, disproving Hensley’s sufficiency claim.

claim_reviews.claim: GPU run for A={1,2,3} processed the entire search space in 12 h on a single DGX node.
claim_reviews.evidence: No performance logs, code-branch description, or wall-clock traces are supplied; given the workload estimate (~10^{14} states) the hardware claim is doubtful without further data.
claim_reviews.concerns: Provide detailed profiling output, GPU count, and algorithmic reduction statistics.

overall_assessment: Most theoretical interpretations align with the literature and the low-range computational data (N≤10^{6}) is sound. However, the headline claim that {1,2,3} is fully covered up to 10^{10} relies on a runtime that appears several orders of magnitude too optimistic, and one Hausdorff-dimension figure is inconsistent. The manuscript needs a transparent accounting of algorithmic complexity, hardware used, and reconciliation of conflicting numerical values.
recommendations: Publish precise runtime logs, GPU counts, and per-kernel throughput for the 10^{10} run.
Document pruning or symmetry-reduction strategies that cut the expected 10^{14} search space to a tractable size.
Fix the conflicting Hausdorff dimension of A={2,3,4,5} and ensure all tables are numerically consistent.
Release bitset output (or its hash) so that independent teams can verify the list of 27 exceptions.
Add a comparison with existing partial-quotient enumeration benchmarks (e.g. Pollicott–Jenkinson codebase) to contextualise speedups.
certification_justification: The experimental framework is grounded in published methods (Hensley 1992; Bourgain–Kontorovich 2014) and most auxiliary claims reproduce known results, but the flagship computation lacks sufficient evidence for gold certification.
